--- PIP 18 - Star Atlas COMET Marketing Expansion ---
[00:00:00] Welcome back to the Hologram News

[00:00:02] Network, your trusted source on the Star

[00:00:04] Atlas political landscape. Today, we're

[00:00:06] covering an essential community funding

[00:00:10] structure and financial support for a

[00:00:12] high-profile realworld event, the Star

[00:00:15] Atlas Comet Community Meetup during

[00:00:16] Gamescom 2025 in Germany. This measure

[00:00:19] was all about increasing the project's

[00:00:21] visibility in the global gaming industry

[00:00:23] and strengthening player bonds. PIP 18

[00:00:26] titled funding for Star Atlas Comet

[00:00:28] Community Meetup at Gamescom 2025

[00:00:31] requested funds from the Dow Treasury to

[00:00:34] expand the reach and impact of the

[00:00:35] communitydriven meetup. The proposal

[00:00:37] aimed to secure €5,775

[00:00:41] paid in the equivalent value of Atlas to

[00:00:44] be dispersed by the DAO. The core

[00:00:46] motivation was to establish the Star

[00:00:48] Atlas Comet as a permanent event in the

[00:00:51] community's annual calendar, leverage

[00:00:53] the massive audience of Gamescom, the

[00:00:55] world's largest gaming trade fair, and

[00:00:57] attract new players. The funds were used

[00:00:59] to organize the Comet Meetup, which took

[00:01:01] place on Saturday, August the 23rd, 2025

[00:01:04] in Cologne, Germany. The event was held

[00:01:07] at the Quarter 1 venue, a centrallylo

[00:01:09] spot easily accessible from the main

[00:01:11] Gamescom Expo grounds. The planning

[00:01:13] included complimentary catering offering

[00:01:15] finger food, drinks, and an exciting

[00:01:18] program that invited all-star Atlas

[00:01:19] projects and DAXs to participate. The

[00:01:22] maximum number of participants was

[00:01:23] capped at 100 to ensure proper

[00:01:25] organization, and entry was completely

[00:01:27] free for those who pre-registered. The

[00:01:29] DAO's support made it possible to offer

[00:01:31] food, beverages, and a program complete

[00:01:34] with presentations and music, turning a

[00:01:36] simple gathering into a highquality

[00:01:38] community experience. The effort paid

[00:01:40] off. The comet meetup was deemed a great

[00:01:42] success by attendees, drawing around 70

[00:01:45] participants. Attendees included many

[00:01:47] existing community members meeting in

[00:01:49] person for the first time, but

[00:01:50] importantly, many new faces visited,

[00:01:53] curious to learn more about Star Atlas

[00:01:54] and its community. Two team members from

[00:01:57] ATMTA even traveled from abroad to

[00:02:00] importance. The event featured

[00:02:02] presentations, music, good vibes, and

[00:02:05] concluded with a raffle where every

[00:02:07] attendee went home with a prize. The

[00:02:09] Dow's funding achieved its goal of

[00:02:10] creating a highly visible, positive, and

[00:02:12] inclusive on-ramp for new players during

[00:02:15] a major Global Gaming Week. While the

[00:02:17] main Gamescom expo ran from August 20th

--- Star Atlas PIP 19 - Economic Research and Data Funding ---
[00:00:00] Welcome back to the Hologram News

[00:00:01] Network, your trusted source on the Star

[00:00:03] Atlas political landscape. Today, we're

[00:00:06] providing an in-depth post-mortem on the

[00:00:08] failure of PIP 19, funding independent

[00:00:11] economic research and maintenance of

[00:00:12] critical data resources, a massive

[00:00:14] multi-phase proposal that divided the

[00:00:16] DAO, and ultimately failed to secure

[00:00:19] approval. This initiative designed to

[00:00:21] provide investors and DAXs with crucial

[00:00:23] transparency and data was narrowly

[00:00:25] defeated, leaving a significant gap in

[00:00:27] the ecosystem's planned economic

[00:00:29] infrastructure. The sheer ambition of

[00:00:32] this plan makes its failure notable. The

[00:00:34] entire program sought 45 to 800,000

[00:00:36] atlas over 360 days distributed as four

[00:00:40] sub PAPs with the first being PIP 19A at

[00:00:43] 11-200,000 atlas. The author is Steven

[00:00:46] Sabile, known as Datab Bros, a highly

[00:00:48] qualified figure with extensive

[00:00:50] experience in structural economics.

[00:00:52] Before submitting this proposal, he

[00:00:54] served as the director of metverse

[00:00:56] economics at Star Atlas and prior to

[00:00:58] that had a proven track record,

[00:01:00] including time at the Federal Reserve.

[00:01:02] This was not a submission from a novice.

[00:01:04] It was a proposal for continued

[00:01:05] independent open-source economic

[00:01:07] research led by a seasoned expert. The

[00:01:10] core motivation of the proposal was to

[00:01:12] establish long-term stability. The

[00:01:14] funding was critical to cover the recent

[00:01:16] increased computational cost of quering

[00:01:18] blockchain data. The plan required

[00:01:20] migrating data resources over to

[00:01:21] Snowflake from the former Flip Side

[00:01:23] Studio platform. Over 44% of the initial

[00:01:26] funding for PIP 19A was designated for

[00:01:29] Snowflake credits and AWS cloud

[00:01:31] services. Without this funding, the

[00:01:33] long-term maintenance of the public data

[00:01:35] resources remains at risk. The defeat of

[00:01:38] PIP 19 means the community has lost

[00:01:40] access to several key economic

[00:01:41] deliverables that were planned to start

[00:01:43] almost immediately. The proposal

[00:01:45] included a Star Atlas monthly

[00:01:46] tokconomics update that would provide an

[00:01:49] independent written assessment of

[00:01:50] essential trends in Addles and Polus

[00:01:53] delivered as Xthreads and an accessible

[00:01:55] Google sheet. Additionally, the proposed

[00:01:58] DAO dashboard was slated to be updated

[00:02:00] bi-weekly with custom charts and the DAX

[00:02:04] Starbase core earnings report would

[00:02:06] track DAC profits, membership, and

[00:02:08] external revenues every quarter. This

[00:02:11] data was intended to provide

[00:02:12] standardized reporting dashboards to

[00:02:14] ensure equitable access to economic

[00:02:16] performance data, benefiting DAXs

[00:02:18] regardless of their individual

[00:02:20] analytical capacity. The project

[00:02:22] included a one-time process to correctly

[00:02:24] classify and tag over 85 known Star

[00:02:26] Atlas wallets and programs on public

[00:02:29] explorer websites like Dune and Soulcan.

[00:02:32] This tagging was vital for enhanced

[00:02:34] investor research by external and

[00:02:36] blockchain analysts. Lastly, the

[00:02:38] proposal included funding for tax tool

[00:02:40] research. Looking forward, the proposal

[00:02:42] planned for a 2025 year end tax tool, a

[00:02:45] wallet lookup feature for earnings and

[00:02:47] expenses, which is now delayed

[00:02:49] indefinitely. The final vote was

[00:02:51] incredibly close, turning on external

[00:02:53] political pressure and community

[00:02:55] reaction. When the vote was in danger of

[00:02:58] failing, author Steven Sabal wrote

[00:03:00] several angry messages on X posts that

[00:03:03] were quickly deleted. This public

[00:03:05] emotional response had a direct impact

[00:03:07] on governance, causing some supporters

[00:03:09] to switch their vote to no. Despite the

[00:03:11] initial ground swell of support, the

[00:03:13] final tally showed 57.89%

[00:03:16] of PvP voting against. Because the

[00:03:19] measure did not meet the required

[00:03:21] threshold for approval, the DAO

[00:03:23] officially rejected PIP 19. The failure

[00:03:26] of a proposal with this level of

[00:03:27] technical merit, due in part to the

[00:03:29] author's public conduct, underscores

[00:03:32] that in a decentralized system,

[00:03:34] community perception and political

[00:03:36] conduct are as critical as the

[00:03:38] proposal's content. The fate of

[00:03:40] independent economic research remains

[00:03:42] uncertain following this vote. Although

[00:03:44] the recently proposed Rogue data hub

[00:03:46] shows promise to cover some of the

[00:03:47] missing features. As always, stay

[00:03:50] vigilant pilots.


--- Star Atlas PIP 20 - The DAO Caster Program ---
[00:00:00] Welcome back to the hologram news

[00:00:01] network, your trusted source on the Star

[00:00:03] Atlas political landscape. The Star

[00:00:05] Atlas DAO has marked a significant

[00:00:07] milestone with the passage of PIP 20,

[00:00:09] establishing the innovative Dowcasters

[00:00:11] program. This initiative represents a

[00:00:14] strategic move to enhance community

[00:00:15] engagement and content creation within

[00:00:17] the Star Atlas ecosystem, addressing key

[00:00:20] communication needs while maintaining

[00:00:22] efficient governance structures. The

[00:00:24] program conceived by council member

[00:00:26] Signal creates positions for five

[00:00:28] elected content creators who will serve

[00:00:31] as ecosystem ambassadors. This structure

[00:00:33] addresses a crucial need allowing the

[00:00:35] council to concentrate on governance

[00:00:37] while ensuring consistent highquality

[00:00:39] public communication.

[00:00:41] The program's initial scope is

[00:00:42] deliberately focused, running as a

[00:00:44] three-month pilot with any extension

[00:00:46] requiring a completely new proposal and

[00:00:48] community vote, demonstrating a measured

[00:00:51] approach to resource allocation. The

[00:00:53] selection process implements the same

[00:00:55] standards used for DAO council elections

[00:00:57] utilizing single transferable vote STV

[00:01:00] to ensure democratic representation.

[00:01:02] This method ensures that selected

[00:01:04] casters have genuine community backing

[00:01:06] and legitimacy. A robust accountability

[00:01:08] framework allows atmata to pause or

[00:01:11] withhold payments if legitimate

[00:01:13] performance concerns arise, creating a

[00:01:15] balance between creative freedom and

[00:01:17] responsible oversight. Content

[00:01:20] requirements for the casters blend

[00:01:21] structure with creative flexibility.

[00:01:24] Each member must produce at least one

[00:01:25] original piece weekly, which can take

[00:01:27] various forms, including videos,

[00:01:29] articles, streams, or promotional

[00:01:32] campaigns. This diversity and content

[00:01:34] format ensures broad appeal and

[00:01:36] engagement across different audience

[00:01:37] preferences. The monthly Dowcasters Brew

[00:01:40] live stream on Discord serves as a

[00:01:42] crucial connecting point between the

[00:01:43] community and development team,

[00:01:44] fostering transparent communication and

[00:01:46] regular updates. The financial structure

[00:01:48] of the program is comprehensive with a

[00:01:51] total budget of 10,800,000 Atlas

[00:01:54] allocated from the ecosystem fund. This

[00:01:56] funding is divided into two distinct

[00:01:58] monthly payment categories for each

[00:02:00] caster. The first is personal

[00:02:02] compensation providing 360,000 Atlas

[00:02:05] monthly paid after each 30-day service

[00:02:07] period. The second category allocates an

[00:02:10] additional 360,000 Atlas monthly for

[00:02:13] promotional activities specifically

[00:02:15] earmarked for community engagement

[00:02:17] initiatives like contests and giveaways.

[00:02:19] A key aspect of the financial framework

[00:02:22] is its built-in oversight mechanisms.

[00:02:24] Before accessing promotional funds,

[00:02:26] casters must submit detailed promotional

[00:02:28] schedules for approval. This requirement

[00:02:30] ensures strategic use of resources and

[00:02:32] alignment with ecosystem goals while

[00:02:34] maintaining transparency in fund

[00:02:36] allocation and usage. The proposal

[00:02:39] success was decisive with the October

[00:02:41] 15th vote demonstrating strong community

[00:02:43] support. The pre-closing snapshot

[00:02:46] revealed approximately 71 million PVP

[00:02:48] committed with over 77% voting in favor.

[00:02:52] These numbers not only validate the

[00:02:53] program's concept, but also indicate

[00:02:55] broad community recognition of the need

[00:02:57] for enhanced content creation and

[00:02:59] ecosystem growth. The pilot nature of

[00:03:01] the program allows for valuable data

[00:03:03] collection and performance assessment.

[00:03:05] This evaluation period will inform

[00:03:07] potential future iterations, ensuring

[00:03:09] that any subsequent versions can be

[00:03:11] optimized based on real world

[00:03:13] performance and community feedback. This

[00:03:15] approach demonstrates a commitment to

[00:03:16] both innovation and responsible resource

[00:03:18] management within the Star Atlas

[00:03:20] ecosystem. The implementation of PIP 20

[00:03:23] marks a pivotal moment in Star Atlas's

[00:03:25] development, creating a structured

[00:03:27] framework for community-driven content

[00:03:29] creation while maintaining professional

[00:03:31] standards and accountability. Through

[00:03:33] this program, Star Atlas is positioning

[00:03:35] itself for enhanced community

[00:03:37] engagement, more effective

[00:03:38] communication, and sustainable ecosystem

[00:03:40] growth, all while ensuring responsible

[00:03:43] use of community resources and

[00:03:44] maintaining democratic principles in its

[00:03:46] governance structure. As always, H&N

[00:03:49] encourages every citizen to lock their

[00:03:51] polls and cast their vote because in the

[00:03:54] Galia expanse, your PvP is your voice.

[00:03:57] We'll see you on the next update.

--- Star Atlas PIP 21 Overview and Vote Results ---
[00:00:00] Welcome back to HNN, your hologram news

[00:00:02] network for the Galia Expanse political

[00:00:04] landscape. Today we bring you the

[00:00:07] official final results and summary of a

[00:00:08] critical community governance proposal,

[00:00:10] PIP 21, the rogue data hub or RDH.

[00:00:16] This proposal put forth by the SNB

[00:00:18] community requested funding from the

[00:00:20] DAO's ecosystem fund to establish a

[00:00:23] reliable open-source data infrastructure

[00:00:25] for Star Atlas. The goal was to build a

[00:00:28] communityrun indexer capable of

[00:00:30] collecting, decoding, and serving

[00:00:32] real-time economic data from the Salana

[00:00:34] blockchain, addressing the urgent need

[00:00:37] for a replacement following the stoppage

[00:00:39] of a previous data service.

[00:00:42] The team demonstrated a working minimum

[00:00:44] viable product or MVP, which was already

[00:00:47] live and featured asset lookup and

[00:00:50] marketplace history functions.

[00:00:52] Proponents argued that this solution was

[00:00:54] necessary to ensure the community's

[00:00:56] continued access to economic data. They

[00:00:59] highlighted the team's technical

[00:01:01] expertise and professionalism, pointing

[00:01:03] to prior successful work. They also

[00:01:06] emphasized the proposal's long-term

[00:01:08] sustainability and revenue sharing

[00:01:10] potential for the DAO, mitigating the

[00:01:12] approximately $8,800

[00:01:14] funding request as an acceptable risk

[00:01:16] for the value provided. Crucially, the

[00:01:19] development team affirmed that the

[00:01:21] system was flexible enough to manage any

[00:01:23] upcoming C4 game changes, ensuring that

[00:01:26] the fundamental database and indexer

[00:01:28] work would not need to be redone.

[00:01:32] The opposition, primarily composed of

[00:01:33] large Polish voting power holders,

[00:01:35] voiced two key concerns. The first was

[00:01:38] project timing, arguing that the

[00:01:40] proposal should have been postponed

[00:01:42] until after the imminent C4 game

[00:01:44] release, which could affect the

[00:01:45] technical scope.

[00:01:47] The second more political concern

[00:01:49] focused on a potential conflict of

[00:01:51] interest, arguing that the SNB team

[00:01:53] already runs a private escrow service,

[00:01:56] which some view as a competitor to the

[00:01:58] DAO by diverting transaction fees. This

[00:02:01] opposition questioned funding a group

[00:02:03] whose prior product operates outside the

[00:02:06] DAO's direct revenue model. Despite the

[00:02:08] significant debate and a concerted no

[00:02:11] effort from a block of large wallets,

[00:02:13] PP21 has officially passed. The

[00:02:16] community driven by a need for data

[00:02:18] transparency and a working community

[00:02:20] solution mobilized enough support to

[00:02:22] secure the win giries. The final vote

[00:02:24] tally was 77 million PvP in support

[00:02:27] versus 55 million PVP against the

[00:02:30] passage of PIP21 directs the DAO to move

[00:02:33] forward with funding the rogue data hub

[00:02:36] cementing a critical piece of community

[00:02:37] controlled infrastructure for the Star

[00:02:39] Atlas ecosystem.

[00:02:42] That's the latest in DAO governance. As

[00:02:45] always, H&N encourages every citizen to

[00:02:47] lock their polls and cast their vote

[00:02:49] because in the Gallia Expanse, your PVP

[00:02:51] is your voice. Stay vigilant, pilots.

[00:02:54] We'll see you on the next update.
